rootText: Manopubbangamā dhammā manoseṭṭhā manomayā, manasā ce paduṭṭhena bhāsati vā karoti vā, tato nam dukkham anveti cakkaṃ va vahato padaṃ.
translationText: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel of the cart follows the foot of the ox that draws the cart.

rootText: Manopubbangamā dhammā manoseṭṭhā manomayā, manasā ce pasannena bhāsati vā karoti vā, tato nam sukham anveti chāyā va anapāyino.
translationText: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.

rootText: Akkocchi mam avadhi mam ajini mam ahāsi me, ye tam upanayhanti veram tesam na sammati.
translationText: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me," in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.

rootText: Na hi verena verāni sammantīdha kudācanaṃ, averena ca sammantī esa dhammo sanantano.
translationText: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me," in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease. For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love — this is an old rule.

rootText: Na tena bhikkhu so hoti yāvataṃ bhūsako hoti, yo ca hāsi ca sīlassa, upeto paññavepamano.
translationText: He is not a mendicant who only asks for alms; but he who is above good and evil, and leads a life of chastity and knowledge, is rightly called a mendicant.

rootText: Cittam na me nivattissati ciraṃ amogham ujjhaggham tāni cittassupassenā vā jāṇāmi.
translationText: The mind is wavering and restless, difficult to guard, difficult to restrain. The wise man makes it straight, as a fletcher makes straight an arrow.

rootText: Yathā vārivahū pothī, na sakkoti siraṃ ḍayhaṃ, na sakkoti saṃkuñcitvā, sarīraṃ tassa medakaṃ.
translationText: Like a fish drawn out of the water and thrown on the dry ground, our mind quivers and trembles, seeking to escape from the dominion of Māra (the tempter).

rootText: Anagghyaṃ esā dhammāno padhāya jinacchaṃ, yogakkhemam anuttaraṃ sambodhiyā gavesī.
translationText: They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, will never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires. They who know truth as truth, and untruth as untruth, will arrive at truth, and follow true desires.

rootText: Idam avoca bhagavā. Attamanā pañcavaggiyā bhikkhū bhagavato bhāsitaṃ abhinanduṃ.
translationText: This is the noble truth of suffering (dukkha): birth is suffering, aging is suffering, illness is suffering, death is suffering; union with what is displeasing is suffering; separation from what is pleasing is suffering; not to get what one wants is suffering; in brief, the five aggregates of clinging are suffering.

This is the noble truth of the origin (samudaya) of suffering: it is craving (taṇhā) — craving for sensual pleasure, craving for existence, craving for non-existence — which leads to renewed becoming, delighting now here, now there.

This is the noble truth of the cessation (nirodha) of suffering: it is the complete fading away and cessation of that very craving (taṇhā), giving it up, relinquishing it, letting it go, freedom from it, non-reliance on it.

This is the noble truth of the way (magga) leading to the cessation of suffering: it is the Noble Eightfold Path — right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness (sati), right concentration (samādhi).
